title: Lesson 409
transcript: So we just walked through all of these different key measurements of time, RPO, RTO, WRT and MTD, right? These ones, we just walked through all of these. Now I can tell you from experience that many people struggle with understanding the difference between these, especially RPO and RTO. Myself included. It's a very subtle difference between these. And I recently came across something that I think really helps solidify, at least for me, so I hope it does for you. It really helps solidify the difference between RPO and RTO. So I wanted to add this in here and show you this. RPO and RTO are like asking these questions. So RPO is like asking, how old can the restored data be? Can it be a few seconds old, a few minutes old, a few hours old, et cetera. So if the business owner says to you, if my system fails, and when you recover my system, I'm only willing to lose two seconds worth of data, then would it be, like if your sort of recovery solution was that you were doing a nightly backup every 24 hours, are you going to be able to meet the RPO, right? Because if the owner says, I'm only willing to lose two seconds worth of data, you're essentially saying your RPO is two seconds, right? Because the RPO is a measurement of time. So if the owner says, my RPO is two seconds, if you're doing a nightly backup every 24 hours, are you going to be able to meet this RPO No, right? Because you could potentially lose up to 24 hours worth of data if you're only backing up every night. So if the owner says, I'm only willing to lose two seconds worth of data, then you're going to have to shift to a much more expensive, probably some sort of mere real-time streaming mirror backup or something like that, or a real-time replication of the data to a secondary database or something like that. So just remember, a really good way of remembering RPO is asking this question, how old can the restored data be? And then RTO is asking this question, how fast does the data need to be restored? Like, am I okay if it takes two hours to restore the data? Or like the owner should say this, right? Is the owner saying, you know what? I'm okay if it takes two hours. Or you know what? I'm okay if it takes two days to restore the data. Again, this is going to drive different recovery solutions. if the owner says i need that data to be restored within one minute you're gonna have to implement probably some sort of like active active solution where you know the redundant you know there's like a redundant system and it comes on you or maybe a good example would be like a hot spare we'll talk did we talk about these yeah we did we talked about hot spares earlier in domain seven So this would require, say, like a hot spare so that if the primary something failed, you had a hot spare there could automatically come online and you could get the system restored, the data back up, whatever, within, say, two seconds. So that's what Archie was asking. How fast does it have to be restored? The other thing I'll say to these is these have huge cost implications. The more stringent these requires are, and by stringent we mean less time. So if the owner says, I need this up in seconds, or I need to only lose seconds worth of data, that's a more stringent requirement, that's a higher requirement, that's gonna cost more money. So if the owner is willing to make their requirement less stringent, if they're able to essentially lower their requirement, if they're able to say, you know what? You can restore my data in 10 hours or three days. They're actually lowering these RPO or RTRO numbers. That's actually going to help to save costs. Because by making the requirement less stringent, you can implement cheaper recovery solutions. If the requirement is more stringent, in other words, less time, then you have to implement much more expensive solutions. Getting owners to basically lower these RPO or RTO numbers can help save a lot of money in the types of recovery solutions that you need to implement. So there we go. I hope this helps you. How old does the restored data need to be? How fast does it need to be restored?
